summary: sensivity ships obfuscated loader, hidden persistence as fake OneDrive updater, and remote-controlled native binary
details: The package combines several supply-chain attack ingredients. launcher.js writes an HKCU Run key named 'OneDriveUpdate' pointing at a shipped VBS (OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs) that silently launches `node launcher.js` with show=0 on every Windows login — persistence under a Microsoft-impersonating identity. launcher.js then reads server.obf.js (a heavily obfuscated obfuscator.io/RC4 bundle, 826-entry string array) and runs it through `eval(fs.readFileSync(...))`. The decoded server collects a host fingerprint built from the registry MachineGuid (read via PowerShell from HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography), os.hostname(), and the C: volume serial number, SHA-256s them as `hwid`, and POSTs JSON {key, hwid, nonce, app:'forte', version} to a hardcoded LICENSE_URL. The package also ships sens.node, a 6.86 MB undocumented native addon loaded as `forte` and driven by socket.io commands (`forte.start`, `forte.stop`, `forte.performAction(name, args)`, `forte.setConfiguration`). launcher.js actively rewrites stack traces to mask the binary's name (`.replace(/sens\.node/gi, 'program module')`). The cumulative behavior — eval of an obfuscated blob, hidden Microsoft-impersonating autostart, host-fingerprint beacon to an author endpoint, and a remote-commanded opaque native binary in the installer's node process — is a backdoor pattern. Persistence and the native-runtime command channel fire when the user runs the package's CLI/`npm start`.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
